items.title: Fake recruiter campaign targets crypto developers with RAT
items.description: A sophisticated fake recruitment campaign named 'graphalgo' has been active since May 2025, targeting JavaScript and Python developers in the cryptocurrency sector. Attackers approach victims through LinkedIn, Facebook, and Reddit with fabricated job opportunities from fake blockchain companies like Veltrix Capital. The campaign uses malicious dependencies hidden in npm and PyPI packages, delivere
items.ai_summary: The 'graphalgo' campaign uses social engineering to target crypto developers with malicious npm and PyPI packages, delivering Remote Access Trojans (RATs) for unauthorized access. This threat has real-world impact as it compromises developer systems, potentially leading to source code and sensitive data theft. Defenders should prioritize verifying the authenticity of job opportunities and inspecting dependencies in development packages.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1082| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Live C2 Dump Recovering Every Stage of the Kill Chain: CHM Dropper, VBScript Stager, PowerShell Keylogger
items.description: On April 11, 2026, researchers analyzed a CHM file (api_reference.chm) tagged as Kimsuky that initiated a three-stage attack chain. The C2 server at check[.]nid-log[.]com had directory listing enabled, allowing recovery of complete source code for all payload stages: a 6,338-byte VBScript performing system reconnaissance and establishing persistence via scheduled task, a 449-byte VBScript bridge t
items.ai_summary: The Kimsuky threat actor utilizes a three-stage attack chain initiated by a CHM dropper, leveraging VBScript and PowerShell to establish persistence and capture input. This attack chain enables the threat actor to perform system reconnaissance, establish scheduled tasks, and capture sensitive information. To defend against this threat, it is crucial to monitor for suspicious scheduled tasks and PowerShell activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1053| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1056| E[Collection]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Polymarket Trader Funds at Risk: DPRK npm Package Steals Wallet Keys and Installs SSH Backdoor
items.description: On April 10, 2026, a malicious npm package named sleek-pretty@1.0.0 was published, targeting developers running automated trading bots on Polymarket, a prediction market platform with $477 million in open interest. The package executes four attack chains upon import: system fingerprinting, SSH backdoor installation on Linux hosts, filesystem exfiltration, and targeted theft of Polymarket CLOB API
items.ai_summary: A malicious npm package named sleek-pretty@1.0.0 was published on April 10, 2026, targeting Polymarket developers. The package executes four attack chains, including system fingerprinting, SSH backdoor installation, and theft of Polymarket CLOB API keys. Defenders should immediately remove the package and update their dependencies.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1592| B[Gather Victim Host Info]:::tactic
    B -->|T1195.001| C[Compromise Software Dependencies]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Exfiltration Over C2 Channel]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Q1 2026 malware statistics report for Windows web servers
items.description: Analysis of Windows web server attacks during Q1 2026 reveals that Internet Information Services (IIS) and Apache Tomcat servers face persistent threats through web shell exploitation. The Larva-26001 threat actor has been targeting domestic IIS servers for several years, deploying privilege escalation tools including JuicyPotato, BadPotato, and exploiting CVE-2019-1458. Following privilege escala
items.ai_summary: The Larva-26001 threat actor exploits web shell vulnerabilities in IIS and Apache Tomcat servers to gain privilege escalation, using tools like JuicyPotato and BadPotato. This allows them to maintain persistent access to domestic IIS servers. Defenders should prioritize patching and monitoring for suspicious activity related to these vulnerabilities.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1505| B[Server Software Component]:::tactic
    B -->|T1068| C[Exploitation for Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    C -->|T1649| D[Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates]:::tactic
    D -->|T1059.001| E[Execution]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: March 2026 CVE Landscape: 31 High-Impact Vulnerabilities Identified, Interlock Ransomware Group Exploits Cisco FMC Zero-Day
items.description: In March 2026, 31 high-impact vulnerabilities were identified requiring prioritization for remediation, with 29 receiving Very Critical Risk Scores. Affected vendors included Cisco, Microsoft, Google, ConnectWise, and others, with Microsoft and Apple accounting for approximately 32% of vulnerabilities. Notably, the Interlock Ransomware Group exploited CVE-2026-20131, a zero-day deserialization vul
items.ai_summary: The Interlock Ransomware Group exploited a zero-day deserialization vulnerability (CVE-2026-20131) in Cisco FMC, using T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application) and T1486 (Data Encrypted for Impact) tactics. This attack resulted in a high-impact data breach. Defenders should prioritize remediation of the 31 identified high-impact vulnerabilities.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1486| C[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Q1 2026 Malware Statistics Report for Windows Database Servers
items.description: During the first quarter of 2026, Windows-based MS-SQL and MySQL database servers experienced consistent malicious attacks with a temporary decrease in February before rising again in March. The primary threat actor, Larva-26002, leveraged various utilities including BCP, curl, bitsadmin, and PowerShell to deploy a Go-based scanner called ICE Cloud, which contained Turkish language strings and C&C
items.ai_summary: Larva-26002 threat actors exploited Windows database servers using PowerShell, BITS Jobs, and Application Layer Protocol to deploy a Go-based scanner called ICE Cloud. This attack resulted in malicious activity on MS-SQL and MySQL servers. Defenders should monitor for suspicious PowerShell and BITS Jobs activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1197| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Q1 2026 Malware Statistics Report for Linux SSH Servers
items.description: Analysis of attacks against Linux SSH servers during Q1 2026 reveals P2PInfect worm as the dominant threat, representing 70.3% of all attack sources. DDoS botnets including Mirai, XMRig, Prometei, and CoinMiner were identified as primary threats. A notable campaign involved installing V2Ray proxy tools on compromised systems, attributed to a suspected Chinese threat actor. Attackers employed SSH b
items.ai_summary: The P2PInfect worm dominates attacks on Linux SSH servers in Q1 2026, with 70.3% of all attack sources. Attackers employ T1583.005 (Botnet), T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application), T1496 (Resource Hijacking), and T1498 (Network Denial of Service) tactics. Defenders should prioritize patching and monitoring SSH servers for signs of exploitation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1583.005| C[Botnet]:::tactic
    C -->|T1496| D[Resource Hijacking]:::tactic
    D -->|T1498| E[Network Denial of Service]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Pretexting-Based Targeted Intrusion: Analysis of Facebook Reconnaissance and Software Tampering Attacks
items.description: APT37 conducted a sophisticated social engineering campaign utilizing Facebook accounts claiming locations in Pyongyang and Pyongsong, North Korea, to conduct reconnaissance and build trust with targets. After establishing relationships through Facebook Messenger, the threat actor migrated conversations to Telegram and employed pretexting tactics, claiming to share encrypted PDF documents containi
items.ai_summary: The threat involves APT37 conducting social engineering campaigns through Facebook and Telegram, utilizing pretexting tactics to build trust with targets and potentially gain access to sensitive information. The real-world impact includes compromised user data and potential software tampering. The most important defender action is to educate users about social engineering tactics and implement robust verification processes for online interactions.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1593.001| B[Reconnaissance]:::tactic
    B -->|T1598| C[Initial Access]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1055| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: 59 Victims, Zero Authentication: A ClickFix Campaign Force-Installs a Chrome Extension Banking Stealer and Leaves the Entire C2 Wide Open
items.description: A Brazilian banking fraud operation leveraging ClickFix social engineering was discovered through a community tip, exposing a completely unauthenticated command-and-control infrastructure. The campaign deploys a malicious Chrome extension masquerading as a Banco Central do Brasil tool, force-installed via Chrome Cloud Management enrollment tokens. The extension achieves zero antivirus detections w
items.ai_summary: A Brazilian banking fraud operation uses ClickFix social engineering to force-install a malicious Chrome extension, achieving zero antivirus detections. The extension is deployed via Chrome Cloud Management enrollment tokens, and the C2 infrastructure is left unauthenticated. Defenders should prioritize patching Chrome Cloud Management and monitoring for suspicious extension activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[User Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1036| D[Masquerading]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Application Layer Protocol]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: 108 Chrome Extensions Linked to Data Exfiltration and Session Theft via Shared C2 Infrastructure
items.description: A coordinated campaign of 108 malicious Chrome extensions operated through shared command-and-control infrastructure at cloudapi[.]stream has been identified, collectively accounting for approximately 20,000 installations. The campaign spans multiple threat categories: 54 extensions steal Google account identities via OAuth2, one extension actively exfiltrates Telegram Web sessions every 15 second
items.ai_summary: A coordinated campaign of 108 malicious Chrome extensions steals Google account identities and exfiltrates Telegram Web sessions via shared C2 infrastructure. The campaign has approximately 20,000 installations and spans multiple threat categories. Defenders should immediately remove the malicious extensions and block the C2 infrastructure at cloudapi[.]stream.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1583| B[Acquire Infrastructure]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Application Layer Protocol]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Exfiltration Over C2 Channel]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-5536: A weakness has been identified in FedML-AI FedML up to 0.8.9. Affected is the fu...
items.description: A weakness has been identified in FedML-AI FedML up to 0.8.9. Affected is the function sendMessage of the file grpc_server.py of the component gRPC server. Executing a manipulation can lead to deserialization. The attack may be performed from remote. The vendor was contacted early about this disclosure but did not respond in any way.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-5536 is a deserialization vulnerability in FedML-AI FedML up to 0.8.9, exploitable from remote, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary code. This weakness has a CVSS score of 7.3, indicating a high severity. Defenders should prioritize patching this vulnerability to prevent exploitation.

items.title: CVE-2026-5535: A security flaw has been discovered in FedML-AI FedML up to 0.8.9. This impacts ...
items.description: A security flaw has been discovered in FedML-AI FedML up to 0.8.9. This impacts an unknown function of the file FileUtils.java of the component MQTT Message Handler. Performing a manipulation of the argument dataSet results in path traversal. The attack is possible to be carried out remotely. The exploit has been released to the public and may be used for attacks. The vendor was contacted early ab
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-5535 is a security flaw in FedML-AI FedML up to 0.8.9, allowing remote path traversal attacks via the dataSet argument in FileUtils.java. This vulnerability has been publicly disclosed and can be exploited using T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application). Defenders should update FedML-AI to a patched version to prevent attacks.

items.title: CVE-2026-5534: A vulnerability was identified in itsourcecode Online Enrollment System 1.0. Thi...
items.description: A vulnerability was identified in itsourcecode Online Enrollment System 1.0. This affects an unknown function of the file /sms/user/index.php?view=edit&id=10 of the component Parameter Handler. Such manipulation of the argument USERID leads to sql injection. The attack can be executed remotely. The exploit is publicly available and might be used.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-5534 is a publicly exploitable SQL injection vulnerability in itsourcecode Online Enrollment System 1.0. An attacker can remotely manipulate the USERID argument to execute malicious SQL queries. Defenders should immediately update the system to a patched version.

The impacted element is an unknown function of the file packages/web-ui/src/tools/artifacts/SvgArtifact.ts of the component SVG Artifact Handler. This manipulation causes cross site scripting. Remote exploitation of the attack is possible. The exploit has been publicly disclosed and may be utilized. The vendor was contacted early about thi
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-5533 is a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability in badlogic pi-mono 0.58.4, allowing remote exploitation. The vulnerability has been publicly disclosed, increasing the risk of exploitation. Defenders should update the affected component to prevent exploitation.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: Analysis of 216M Security Findings Shows a 4x Increase In Critical Risk (2026 Report)
items.description: OX Security recently analyzed 216 million security findings across 250 organizations over a 90-day period. The primary takeaway: while raw alert volume grew by 52% year-over-year, prioritized critical risk grew by nearly 400%. items.title: Scans for EncystPHP Webshell, (Mon, Apr 13th)
items.description: Last week, I wrote about attackers scanning for various webshells, hoping to find some that do not require authentication or others that use well-known credentials. But some attackers are paying attention and are deploying webshells with more difficult-to-guess credentials. Today, I noticed some scans for what appears to be the "EncystPHP" web shell. Fortinet wrote about this webshell back in January. It appears to be a favorite among attackers compromising vulnerable FreePBX systems.

items.title: Number Usage in Passwords: Take Two, (Thu, Apr 9th)
items.description: In a previous diary [1], we looked to see how numbers were used within passwords submitted to honeypots. One of the items of interest was how dates, and more specifically years, were represented within the data and how that changed over time. It is often seen that years and seasons are used in passwords, especially when password change requirements include frequenty password changes. Some examples we might see today:

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday, March 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft Corp. today pushed security updates to fix at least 77 vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and other software. There are no pressing "zero-day" flaws this month (compared to February's five zero-day treat), but as usual some patches may deserve more rapid attention from organizations using Windows. Here are a few highlights from this month's Patch Tuesday.

items.title: How AI Assistants are Moving the Security Goalposts
items.description: AI-based assistants or "agents" -- autonomous programs that have access to the user's computer, files, online services and can automate virtually any task -- are growing in popularity with developers and IT workers. But as so many eyebrow-raising headlines over the past few weeks have shown, these powerful and assertive new tools are rapidly shifting the security priorities for organizations, while blurring the lines between data and code, trusted co-worker and insider threat, ninja hacker and novice code jockey.
items.workflow_graph:

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Who is the Kimwolf Botmaster “Dort”?
items.description: In early January 2026, KrebsOnSecurity revealed how a security researcher disclosed a vulnerability that was used to assemble Kimwolf, the world's largest and most disruptive botnet. Since then, the person in control of Kimwolf -- who goes by the handle "Dort" -- has coordinated a barrage of distributed denial-of-service (DDoS), doxing and email flooding attacks against the researcher and this author, and more recently caused a SWAT team to be sent to the researcher's home. This post examines what is knowable about Dort based on public information.

items.title: ‘Starkiller’ Phishing Service Proxies Real Login Pages, MFA
items.description: Most phishing websites are little more than static copies of login pages for popular online destinations, and they are often quickly taken down by anti-abuse activists and security firms. But a stealthy new phishing-as-a-service offering lets customers sidestep both of these pitfalls: It uses cleverly disguised links to load the target brand's real website, and then acts as a relay between the target and the legitimate site -- forwarding the victim's username, password and multi-factor authentication (MFA) code to the legitimate site and returning its responses.
